title: Merge Sort
whenToUse: When you need $O(n\log n)$ sorting with worst-case guarantees, or a reference example of a D&C recurrence $T(n)=2T(n/2)+n$.
keyFormulas: Merge runs in $O(m_1 + m_2)$ time for inputs of size $m_1, m_2$
Splitting into $k$ pieces (constant $k$) still gives $\Theta(n\log n)$
professor:
**Algorithm.**

$$ \texttt{MergeSort}(A): \text{ if } |A| = 1, \text{ return } A.\ \text{Else } A_1 \leftarrow \texttt{MergeSort}(A[1..n/2]),\ A_2 \leftarrow \texttt{MergeSort}(A[n/2+1..n]),\ \text{return } \texttt{Merge}(A_1, A_2). $$

**Merge.** Maintain one pointer into each of the two sorted inputs. Repeatedly transfer the smaller of the two front elements to the output. After one input is exhausted, append the rest of the other.

**Correctness — Merge (loop invariant).** After $i$ steps, the first $i$ output positions contain the $i$ smallest elements overall, in sorted order.

- Base: trivially true when output is empty.
- Step: if the next elements in the two arrays are $a_p$ and $b_q$, then all remaining elements in each array are $\ge$ their front. So $\min(a_p, b_q)$ is the $(i{+}1)$-th smallest overall.

**Correctness — Merge Sort (induction).** Base: 1-element arrays are trivially sorted. Step: by I.H. the two recursive calls return sorted halves; Merge on two sorted halves returns a fully sorted array.

**Runtime recurrence.** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ with $T(1) = 1$. Level $i$ has $2^i$ subproblems each of size $n/2^i$; non-recursive work per subproblem is $n/2^i$; per-level total is $2^i \cdot (n/2^i) = n$. There are $\log_2 n$ internal levels plus $n$ leaves of unit cost. Total: $n \log_2 n + n = \Theta(n \log n)$.

**Disadvantages.**

- Always makes $\Theta(n \log n)$ comparisons, even when the input is already sorted.
- Needs extra output memory in the Merge step (not in-place).

**Variant: $k$-way merge sort.** Split into $k$ equal pieces, recursively sort each, merge them in $O(n)$ time using $k$ pointers. Recurrence $T(n) = k T(n/k) + n$. Level work: $k^i \cdot (n/k^i) = n$. Height: $\log_k n$. Total $n \log_k n = \Theta(n \log n)$ for any constant $k$. So splitting into more pieces does not change the asymptotic.
simplified:
**Split in half → sort each half recursively → merge.** That's it.

**Merge:** two pointers, one per sorted input, repeatedly pick the smaller front element. Linear time.

**Recurrence:** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n = \Theta(n \log n)$. Worst case, no randomization needed.

**Why $k$-way doesn't help:** splitting into $k$ pieces gives $T(n) = k T(n/k) + n$, still $\Theta(n \log n)$ for any constant $k$. Each level still does $n$ work and there are still logarithmically many levels (just in a different base).

**Downsides:** not in-place (needs extra memory); doesn't exploit already-sorted input.
